title: File Handling
description: File handling at FAANG goes beyond basic read/write. Context managers, large file processing, serialization formats, and memory-mapped files are all interview-relevant for backend and data engineering roles.

sections.title: Context Managers — The Right Way
sections.description: Always use with for file operations. It guarantees the file is closed even if an exception occurs — the context manager's __exit__ is called on any exit path. Never rely on the garbage collector to close files.
sections.codeBlock:
# Basic read/write
with open('data.txt', 'r', encoding='utf-8') as f:
    content = f.read()          # entire file as string

with open('data.txt', 'r') as f:
    lines = f.readlines()       # list of lines with \n

with open('data.txt', 'r') as f:
    for line in f:              # generator — O(1) memory
        process(line.strip())

# Write modes
with open('out.txt', 'w') as f:   # truncate + write
    f.write('hello\n')

with open('out.txt', 'a') as f:   # append
    f.write('world\n')

with open('data.bin', 'wb') as f:  # binary write
    f.write(b'\x00\x01\x02')

# Multiple files in one with
with open('input.txt') as fin, open('output.txt', 'w') as fout:
    for line in fin:
        fout.write(line.upper())

sections.title: Custom Context Managers
sections.description: Write your own context managers using __enter__/__exit__ or the contextlib.contextmanager decorator. Common pattern for DB connections, locks, timers, and temporary directories.
sections.codeBlock:
import time
from contextlib import contextmanager, suppress

# Class-based context manager
class Timer:
    def __enter__(self):
        self._start = time.perf_counter()
        return self
    def __exit__(self, exc_type, exc_val, exc_tb):
        self.elapsed = time.perf_counter() - self._start
        return False  # False = don't suppress exceptions

with Timer() as t:
    data = sorted(range(1_000_000))
print(f'Sort took {t.elapsed:.3f}s')

# Generator-based (simpler for one-shot cleanup)
@contextmanager
def temp_directory():
    import tempfile, shutil
    path = tempfile.mkdtemp()
    try:
        yield path          # caller gets the path
    finally:
        shutil.rmtree(path) # always cleanup

with temp_directory() as tmpdir:
    with open(f'{tmpdir}/test.txt', 'w') as f:
        f.write('temp data')
# tmpdir cleaned up automatically

# suppress — ignore specific exceptions
with suppress(FileNotFoundError):
    open('might_not_exist.txt').close()

sections.title: Serialization — JSON, CSV, Pickle
sections.description: Data serialization is fundamental for APIs, caching, and data pipelines. Know when to use each format — JSON for interchange, pickle for Python objects, CSV for tabular data.
sections.codeBlock:
import json, csv, pickle
from pathlib import Path

# JSON — human-readable, cross-language
data = {'name': 'Alice', 'scores': [98, 87, 92], 'active': True}

# Write
with open('data.json', 'w') as f:
    json.dump(data, f, indent=2, ensure_ascii=False)

# Read
with open('data.json') as f:
    loaded = json.load(f)

# String conversion
json_str = json.dumps(data)       # dict → str
parsed = json.loads(json_str)     # str → dict

# CSV — tabular dataows = [{'name': 'Alice', 'age': 30}, {'name': 'Bob', 'age': 25}]
with open('data.csv', 'w', newline='') as f:
    writer = csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=['name', 'age'])
    writer.writeheader()
    writer.writerows(rows)

with open('data.csv') as f:
    for row in csv.DictReader(f):
        print(row)   # each row is a dict

# Pickle — Python-specific binary serialization
# WARNING: never unpickle untrusted data (arbitrary code execution)
with open('model.pkl', 'wb') as f:
    pickle.dump(data, f)
with open('model.pkl', 'rb') as f:
    loaded = pickle.load(f)

sections.title: Large File Processing
sections.description: At FAANG scale, files can be terabytes. Never load the entire file into memory. Use generators, chunked reading, or memory-mapped files.
sections.codeBlock:
import mmap
from pathlib import Path

# 1. Generator: process line by line — O(1) memory
def process_large_file(path):
    with open(path, 'r', encoding='utf-8') as f:
        for line in f:              # file object is a lazy iterator
            yield line.strip()

for line in process_large_file('huge.log'):
    if 'ERROR' in line:
        print(line)

# 2. Chunked binary reading
def read_in_chunks(path, chunk_size=64*1024):  # 64KB chunks
    with open(path, 'rb') as f:
        while chunk := f.read(chunk_size):    # walrus operator (Python 3.8+)
            yield chunk

# 3. Memory-mapped file — random access without loading to RAM
def find_pattern_mmap(path, pattern: bytes):
    with open(path, 'rb') as f:
        with mmap.mmap(f.fileno(), 0, access=mmap.ACCESS_READ) as mm:
            # mm behaves like bytes but is backed by OS page cache
            pos = mm.find(pattern)
            while pos != -1:
                yield pos
                pos = mm.find(pattern, pos + 1)

# pathlib — modern path manipulation (prefer over os.path)
base = Path('/data/logs')
latest = max(base.glob('*.log'), key=lambda p: p.stat().st_mtime)
print(latest.name, latest.stat().st_size)

sections.title: pathlib & os.path Patterns
sections.description: pathlib.Path (Python 3.4+) is the modern, object-oriented API for path manipulation. Prefer it over string concatenation and os.path functions.
sections.codeBlock:
from pathlib import Path
import shutil

# Path construction
base = Path('/Users/alice/projects')
config = base / 'app' / 'config.json'  # / operator joins paths
print(config)       # /Users/alice/projects/app/config.json
print(config.name)  # config.json
print(config.stem)  # config
print(config.suffix)# .json
print(config.parent)# /Users/alice/projects/app

# Common operations
config.exists()          # bool
config.is_file()         # bool
config.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)  # makedirs
config.write_text('{}')
text = config.read_text()
config.unlink()           # delete file

# Glob patterns
python_files = list(Path('.').rglob('*.py'))  # recursive
for f in Path('logs').glob('*.log'):
    if f.stat().st_size > 100_000:
        shutil.move(str(f), 'logs/archive/')

# Read lines as list
lines = Path('data.txt').read_text().splitlines()

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• Reading and Writing Files — Official Python file I/O tutorial
  • pathlib — Object-oriented filesystem paths — Modern Python path handling (preferred over os.path)
  • tempfile — Generate temporary files and directories — Secure temp file handling for interview questions
  • Working With Files in Python — Comprehensive guide
  • Python Cookbook (3rd ed.) — Chapter 5: files and I/O patterns

details:
Binary vs Text Mode: Text mode ('r', 'w') automatically handles newline translation (\r\n on Windows → \n in Python) and encoding/decoding. Binary mode ('rb', 'wb') passes raw bytes without any transformation. Always use binary mode for non-text files (images, PDFs, pickles, network protocols). Always specify encoding= in text mode to avoid platform-dependent defaults.

Atomic writes: To safely update a file (without corruption if the process dies mid-write), write to a temp file in the same directory, then rename: os.replace(tmp_path, target_path). rename/replace on the same filesystem is atomic on POSIX systems.

Interview scenario: 'You have a 500GB log file. Find all lines with ERROR.' → generator + lazy line iteration. 'Count word frequency across 1TB of text.' → MapReduce pattern: generators + Counter + merge. 'Find a specific record by ID in a 10GB binary file.' → mmap or binary search with fixed-size records.
